meta.name: Burn with Inhalation Injury — Preemptive RSI

patient.name: Marcus Reese
patient.chiefComplaint: Burns and smoke inhalation after structure fire rescue

history.hpi: 50-year-old male pulled from a one-story house fire by an engine crew after being trapped in a back bedroom for an estimated 8-10 minutes. He was found prone on the floor, conscious. Now complaining of a sore throat and difficulty swallowing. Voice quality has been worsening since extraction.
history.events: Structure fire in a single-family home. Patient was asleep when fire started in the adjacent kitchen. Attempted to exit but became disoriented in smoke, trapped until engine crew located and extracted him.

scene.dispatch: Medic 3, respond code 3 to 4412 Birchwood Lane for a 50-year-old male, victim of a structure fire, rescued by fire crew, conscious at this time.
scene.location: In front of the patient's home. Structure fire ongoing with suppression in progress. Patient on a tarp at the command post, approximately 100 feet from the structure.
scene.safety: Scene controlled by fire department. Stage upwind of the structure. Air quality acceptable at the command post.
scene.bystanders: Fire captain provides report of entrapment time and location; patient's neighbor is present
visualCues: Adult male seated upright on a tarp, looking anxious
Facial soot and superficial partial-thickness burns to face and neck (~5% TBSA)
Carbonaceous (black) sputum at the lips when he coughs
Hoarse, raspy voice — progressively worse over the past few minutes
Beginning audible stridor on deeper breaths
Burns to bilateral anterior forearms (~4% TBSA partial-thickness)

phases.name: Arrival — Conscious with Inhalation Injury Signs
phases.description: Crew arrives to a conscious, talking patient with multiple sentinel signs of airway burn: carbonaceous sputum, hoarse voice, singed nasal hairs, and developing stridor. He is currently maintaining his own airway but the trajectory is concerning. Pulse oximetry reads 96% which may be falsely reassuring given probable CO exposure.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent currently; hoarse voice; audible inspiratory stridor on deeper breaths; carbonaceous sputum at mouth
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Mildly tachypneic, using accessory muscles modestly; symmetric chest rise; clear lungs

skin.color: face sooty with superficial burns; no central cyanosis

otherFindings: Approximately 9% TBSA partial-thickness burns (face 5%, both forearms 4%)
Hoarse voice getting audibly worse between sentences
SpO2 reads 96% — unreliable due to carboxyhemoglobin
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Able to speak sentences but with rough, raspy voice; 'My throat really hurts and I feel like I can't breathe as good'

expectedActions.action: Apply high-flow oxygen 100% via non-rebreather mask — do not rely on SpO2 reading; treat presumptively for CO exposure
Recognize sentinel signs of airway burn: hoarse voice, stridor, carbonaceous sputum, singed nasal hairs, facial burns — this is a preemptive RSI indication
expectedActions.rationale: Airway burns progress rapidly. Intubation is easier before edema distorts landmarks.

expectedActions.action: Estimate TBSA using Rule of 9s — approximately 9% partial thickness (face 5%, both forearms 4%)
Remove smoldering clothing and jewelry; leave blisters intact; do not apply gels
Cover burns with dry sterile dressings or clean sheet; keep patient warm to prevent hypothermia
Establish two large-bore IVs (avoid burned skin when possible)
Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography, 12-lead EKG
Administer Fentanyl 1 mcg/kg (95 mcg for 95 kg patient) IV for pain; titrate to effect

phases.name: Preemptive RSI Before Airway Closes
phases.description: Voice is now markedly more hoarse. Stridor is continuous at rest. The window for easy intubation is closing. Crew commits to RSI now, while the patient is still cooperative and the airway anatomy is still recognizable.
phases.triggerCondition: Progressive airway symptoms during preparation phase

transitions.condition: RSI performed with confirmed tube placement
RSI delayed greater than 10 minutes with progressive edema

clinicalPresentation.airway: Worsening stridor at rest, visible accessory muscle use; voice barely audible
otherFindings: Stridor now audible across the room
Patient able to lie supine briefly for procedure

expectedActions.action: Verbalize RSI plan with backup (SGA and surgical airway ready); anticipate difficult airway from edema
Preoxygenate with BVM + PEEP 5 cmH2O and apneic oxygenation NC at 15 LPM
Prepare smaller ETT (6.5-7.0 mm) and bougie due to anticipated airway edema
Fentanyl 1 mcg/kg (95 mcg) IV premedication if not already given
Administer Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV push (150 mg for 95 kg patient) as induction agent
Administer Rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV push (95 mg for 95 kg patient) as paralytic
Perform video laryngoscopy intubation; use bougie-first technique; single best-effort attempt
Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography, bilateral breath sounds, absent epigastric sounds

phases.name: Post-Intubation and Burn Resuscitation
phases.description: Tube confirmed. Crew initiates fluid resuscitation per Rule of 10, continues 100% oxygen for CO exposure, and arranges transport to the most appropriate burn/trauma center with ED notification.

clinicalPresentation.airway: 6.5 mm ETT at 22 cm, cuff inflated, secured
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Ventilated with 100% FiO2, symmetric chest rise

expectedActions.action: Post-intubation sedation: Midazolam 0.02 mg/kg (1.9 mg) IV + Fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg (95-190 mcg) IV; titrate RASS -3 to -4
Ventilate at 10-12 breaths/min on 100% FiO2; target EtCO2 35-45 mmHg; continue 100% oxygen for CO toxicity
Initiate LR fluid resuscitation using Rule of 10: 10% TBSA × 10 = 100 mL/hr, plus 100 mL/hr for 10 kg over 80 kg = 200 mL/hr total infusion rate
expectedActions.rationale: Rule of 10: TBSA (round to 10) × 10 = initial mL/hr for 40-80 kg; add 100 mL/hr per 10 kg over 80 kg. TBSA rounded to 10% × 10 = 100, + 100 (for 10 kg over 80) = 200 mL/hr.

expectedActions.action: Consider cyanide toxicity given enclosed-space fire with hemodynamic/neurologic involvement; Hydroxocobalamin 5 g IV over 15 minutes if clinical suspicion and available
Cover burns with dry sterile dressings; keep patient warm — hypothermia risk is significant
Transport to burn center if criteria met (TBSA >10%, face/airway, hands) — notify ED early for trauma/burn team and hyperbaric consideration

phases.name: Branch: Delayed Intubation with Complete Airway Closure
phases.description: The crew chooses to transport and reassess instead of proactively intubating. En route the airway rapidly edematizes and closes. The patient cannot be intubated, cannot be ventilated with BVM, and a cricothyrotomy through facial burns is now the only option.
phases.triggerCondition: Intubation delayed beyond 10 minutes with progression to complete obstruction

clinicalPresentation.airway: Complete upper airway obstruction from edema; unable to pass ETT; unable to BVM effectively
otherFindings: Surgical cricothyrotomy through burned skin required

expectedActions.action: Perform emergent surgical cricothyrotomy through burned skin — no alternative remains
Recognize that preemptive intubation would have prevented this crisis

criticalActions: Recognize sentinel signs of airway burn and commit to preemptive RSI
100% oxygen via NRB to treat presumed CO toxicity (SpO2 unreliable)
Establish two large-bore IVs avoiding burned skin
Perform RSI with Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg + Rocuronium 1 mg/kg, using smaller ETT with bougie
Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography
Initiate Rule of 10 LR fluid resuscitation (200 mL/hr for this patient)
Cover burns with dry sterile dressings and prevent hypothermia
Transport to burn center with early notification
expectedActions: Fentanyl for pain management during preparation
Monitor for cyanide toxicity in an enclosed-space fire victim
bonusActions: Consider hydroxocobalamin 5 g IV for suspected cyanide toxicity
Consider hyperbaric facility if transport time permits and patient meets criteria
Anticipate difficult airway from burn edema and prepare accordingly

learningObjectives: Identify sentinel signs of airway burn and the rationale for preemptive intubation
Apply the MATC Burns protocol including TBSA estimation and Rule of 10 fluid resuscitation
Recognize that SpO2 is unreliable in CO poisoning
Manage concurrent thermal, inhalation, and chemical (CO/CN) injuries
Coordinate transport destination based on burn severity and airway involvement
discussionQuestions: Why does SpO2 read normal in CO poisoning despite significant tissue hypoxia?
How do you decide between a smaller ETT and bougie versus going straight to surgical airway?
When is it appropriate to transport a burn patient to a burn center versus the closest ED?
What findings would make you suspect concurrent cyanide toxicity?
How does the Rule of 10 differ from the Parkland formula and why does prehospital EMS use it?
commonPitfalls: Being reassured by a normal SpO2 in a CO exposure
Delaying intubation because the patient 'looks okay' and losing the airway en route
Using a standard-sized ETT in an edematous airway
Over-resuscitating with fluids early (no shock in first hour from burn alone)
Forgetting hypothermia risk in a burn patient
keyTakeaways: Voice change, stridor, soot, and singed hairs together = commit to RSI now
SpCO — not SpO2 — tells you about CO exposure; treat empirically if enclosed-space fire
Burn patients lose heat fast; keep them warm
Rule of 10 is simple and works for adults 40-80 kg, with +100 mL/hr per 10 kg above
